danger_signals.visual_form: bullets and missiles from ground/air enemies
danger_signals.meaning: direct threat requiring evasion; collision causes loss of life and firepower downgrade

danger_signals.visual_form: colored waves of approaching enemy planes
danger_signals.meaning: escalating threat intensity; waves indicate stage progression and difficulty increase
stationary and mobile threats that spawn projectiles; concentrated firepower zones

danger_signals.visual_form: terrain and enemy aircraft in player's flight path
danger_signals.meaning: environmental and entity-based immediate danger requiring avoidance

safety_signals.visual_form: designated landing zone at stage transitions
safety_signals.meaning: safe point where player restarts if killed; represents progress save and respawn location

safety_signals.visual_form: "S" icons and point bonuses from destroyed enemies
safety_signals.meaning: destroyed enemies are safe targets; item drops indicate successful engagement
resource replenishment; indicates safe weapon availability

safety_signals.visual_form: reaching final runway after all enemies cleared
safety_signals.meaning: safe zone transition; no new threats spawn after reaching designated endpoint

faction_indicators.environmental_markers: runway takeoff/landing zones; white or neutral color scheme typical of arcade player sprites

faction_indicators.color_identity: typically dark/contrasting colors for distinction from player
faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: tanks, battleships, artillery, enemy fighter planes
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: colored waves of enemy planes suggest different enemy unit types or aircraft variants
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: ground-based military installations, scattered across scrolling battlefield terrain

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: sparse enemy patterns; slower projectile speed; simpler enemy wave formations
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: enemies fire at standard rate; first stage designed as tutorial for new players

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: increased density of enemy units on screen; more complex layered enemy waves
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: enemy planes begin aiming shots at player position rather than straight patterns; increased bullet density

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: maximum sprite count; heavy concentration of ground and air units; dense projectile patterns
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: enemy fire becomes predictive; bullet speed increases significantly; checkpoints become critical visual markers

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: second loop maintains visual continuity but introduces subtle difficulty increase markers
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: increased bullet speed returns to normal after overflow; difficulty persists across stage restarts

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: visual feedback on weapon effectiveness; shot range reduction indicated by projectile visual size/distance
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: after hit, plane returns to base shot; reduced damage output until "S" powerups collected

environmental_storytelling.example: left-right scrolling backgrounds convey Thai landscape and warzone geography; constantly moving scenery immerses player in traversal through combat theater (inspired by Apocalypse Now aesthetic)
stages feature escalating military infrastructure: ground positions evolve from simple batteries to battleships and complex fortifications; visual hierarchy communicates enemy resource commitment
five increasing difficulty levels marked by distinct environmental visual signatures; runway zones provide clear narrative checkpoints and safe harbor imagery
tanks, battleships, artillery, and fighter planes represent different military branches; visual diversity communicates realistic war theater with multiple threat vectors

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: full firepower; extended shot range; faster responsiveness; can absorb hits and maintain capability
firepower downgraded to base shot; reduced effective range; must seek "S" powerups to restore capability
limited bombs remaining; must choose bomb deployment carefully; visual indicator of bomb stock displayed
non-predictive fire patterns; simple wave formations; wide spacing between units
aiming behavior activated; shots directed at player position; tighter formation clustering; increased fire rate
consistent outgoing fire; some units spawn powerups when destroyed; stationary positioning with limited movement
clears portion of screen of all bullets; damages enemies within blast radius; visual explosion effect communicates safety window

notes: Sky Shark (NES 1989) communicates world-state primarily through enemy density escalation, projectile speed increase, and behavioral AI changes rather than explicit visual environmental shifts. The game uses military unit type variety and runway checkpoints to structure narrative progression. Danger signals are heavily weighted toward enemy projectiles and wave formations, reflecting the core SHMUP challenge loop. The Apocalypse Now-inspired aesthetic (mentioned in development) was conveyed through Thai landscape scrolling backgrounds, though NES technical limitations constrain visual storytelling. Firepower degradation serves as a critical player-state signal affecting combat viability. The checkpoint/runway system provides both mechanical and visual safety communication.
sources_used: wikipedia - Flying Shark (describes gameplay, enemy types, level progression, development intent)
tcrf - Sky Shark NES (credits/developer metadata; limited gameplay documentation)
